coredata.dc:description: Nitrite (NO2 −) is widely present in the natural environment and human daily life. Excessive NO2 − can cause harm to the environment and human health. Herein, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 70 % were synthesised using a one-pot hydrothermal method and combined with the common and inexpensive o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to achieve the detection of NO2 −. Upon the addition of NO2 −, the blue fluorescence of the SiNPs was quenched due to static quenching and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the yellow fluorescence of benzotriazole, the reaction product of OPD and NO2 −, was enhanced, resulting in the fluorescence color change from blue to yellow. Based on these phenomena, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor integrated with smartphone imaging technology was developed. This sensor is notable for its portability, cost-effectiveness, and satisfactory detection limits (0.016 μM for ratiometric fluorescence and 1.64 μM for smartphone imaging). Importantly, it demonstrates high reliability and practicability in detecting NO2 − in real water and food samples. This broadens the application of SiNPs in the sensing field and introduces new possibilities for NO2 − detection in complex sample matrices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite (NO2 −) is widely present in the natural environment and human daily life. Excessive NO2 − can cause harm to the environment and human health. Herein, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) with a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 70 % were synthesised using a one-pot hydrothermal method and combined with the common and inexpensive o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to achieve the detection of NO2 −. Upon the addition of NO2 −, the blue fluorescence of the SiNPs was quenched due to static quenching and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the yellow fluorescence of benzotriazole, the reaction product of OPD and NO2 −, was enhanced, resulting in the fluorescence color change from blue to yellow. Based on these phenomena, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor integrated with smartphone imaging technology was developed. This sensor is notable for its portability, cost-effectiveness, and satisfactory detection limits (0.016 μM for ratiometric fluorescence and 1.64 μM for smartphone imaging). Importantly, it demonstrates high reliability and practicability in detecting NO2 − in real water and food samples. This broadens the application of SiNPs in the sensing field and introduces new possibilities for NO2 − detection in complex sample matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 −), a form of nitrogen in nature, is commonly found in the natural environment [1–3]. In the food processing industry, it is widely used as a food additive and preservative to increase food color, improve taste, and inhibit bacterial growth [4,5]. However, NO2 − reacts with amides or secondary amines in the stomach to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamines, and will also combine with hemoglobin to form methemoglobin, causing hypoxia in the human body [6,7]. Long-term excessive intake of NO2 − will pose a threat to human health. The World Health Organization limits the maximum allowable amount of NO2 − in drinking water to 3 mg/L [8]. The safe concentration of NO2 − should be controlled to less than 30 mg/kg in food and beverages in China [9]. Therefore, rapid and sensitive NO2 − detection is crucial for environmental protection and food safety. A variety of NO2 − detection methods have been developed, including chromatography [10,11], capillary electrophoresis [12], electrochemistry [13,14], chemiluminescence [15,16], colorimetry [17,18], and fluorescence [19,20]. Among them, the fluorescence method is favored because of its simple operation, fast response, high sensitivity, and visibility [21].

A diversity of fluorescent materials have been developed for the detection of NO2 − [22–26]. Among these fluorescent materials, silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) have attracted much attention due to their low toxicity, high stability, environmental friendliness, excellent optical properties, and good biocompatibility [27]. However, there are few reports on the research of SiNPs in NO2 − detection. Yang et al. successfully synthesized SiNPs using a one-pot method at room temperature and constructed a Fe2+-Phen/SiNPs probe for single-signal fluorescence "turn-on" detection of NO2 − [28]. Although it has been successfully applied to detecting NO2 − in real samples, single-signal fluorescence sensors are susceptible to interference from instrumental or environmental factors and can only present changes in brightness, not chromaticity [29]. In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors overcome these shortcomings and have good self-calibration capabilities, which can eliminate erroneous signal interference from the external environment [30–32]. They can also effectively amplify subtle fluorescence signal changes, thereby significantly improving detection sensitivity and accuracy [33,34]. In addition, the multiple fluorescence signals of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor can provide different fluorescence colors, which provides favorable conditions for visual detection [35]. However, there are currently no reports on utilizing SiNPs to achieve dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence and visual detection of NO2 −. Therefore, it is of great theoretical significance and practical value to develop a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection method based on SiNPs and even extend it to visual detection to achieve on-site quantitative detection of NO2 −.

Visual detection is to realize the analysis of the target by visually identifying the luminous intensity or color change with the naked eye. It has the advantages of simple operation, low cost, and no need for large and expensive instruments. It has been widely used in the fields of analysis and sensing [36,37]. However, due to the limited recognition ability of the naked eye and its subjectivity, visual detection with the naked eye alone is not accurate enough [38,39]. Capturing and analyzing images through smartphone photography and color identification Apps can significantly improve sensitivity and reliability [40]. Therefore, combining visual detection with smartphone imaging technology, a simple, portable, and inexpensive smartphone visual sensing platform is constructed to realize on-site detection and real-time monitoring of the target [41,42]. This not only improves the convenience of detection but also avoids the use of expensive instruments, especially providing a feasible detection solution for some underdeveloped regions [43,44]. Therefore, there is great motivation to develop a simple and practical smartphone imaging detection method for NO2 −.

Herein, blue fluorescent SiNPs with high fluorescence quantum yield were successfully synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal method ( Scheme 1A). The SiNPs were then mixed with o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in an appropriate proportion to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensor and smartphone sensing platform for detecting NO2 − in the environment and food (Scheme 1B). When no NO2 − was added, the sensing system showed blue fluorescence of the SiNPs under excitation at 410 nm. When NO2 − was added, the blue fluorescence of the SiNPs was gradually quenched by static quenching and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the yellow fluorescence of benzotriazole, the reaction product of OPD and NO2 −, was gradually enhanced, causing the fluorescence of the solution to show an obvious color change from blue to yellow. The smartphone was used to capture images in real time and analyze RGB (red, green, and blue channel) information to realize on-site visual detection of NO2 −.

The as-prepared SiNPs/OPD system, integrated with a smart sensing platform, demonstrates superior performance in the following aspects: (1) The SiNPs, with a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 70 %, were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method, exhibiting excellent stability and water solubility. (2) The SiNPs/OPD system, a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor, was easily constructed, featuring self-calibration, high sensitivity, a superior signal-to-noise ratio, and noticeable color changes. (3) The sensor exhibits a fast response, a low detection limit, and excellent selectivity, confirming its reliability and practicality for detecting NO2 − in real water and food samples. (4) Furthermore, a visual, quantitative, and portable smartphone-based sensing platform has been developed, facilitating on-site detection and real-time monitoring of NO2 −. Overall, this study introduces a novel NO2 − detection method that stands apart from most previous single-emission fluorescence sensors. By employing a dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence method combined with portable detection equipment, this work provides a promising new strategy for detecting NO2 − and serves as a potential inspiration for detecting environmental and food contaminants.

N-(2-aminoethyl)−3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (N-APTES) was purchased from Tianjin Solomon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium citrate and glutamic acid (Glu) were purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). o-phenylenediamine (OPD), urea, acetic acid (CH3COOH), boric acid (H3BO3), and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Sodium nitrite and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) was purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Supply and Marketing Company (Tianjin, China). Potassium ferrocyanide was purchased from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, zinc acetate, sodium tetraborate, and other inorganic metal salts were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification.
Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 2.0–12.0) was made from 40 mM H3PO4, CH3COOH, H3BO3, and 0.2 M NaOH solution.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F20 from FEI (USA) and a JEM 2100 F from JEOL (Japan). Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image was obtained using a JEM 2100 F from JEOL (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) was performed using the Apreo S LoVac from FEI (Czech Republic). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer (Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK). Fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Zeta potential was obtained with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK). Fluorescence lifetime and fluorescence quantum yield were measured on an FLS-920 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). The silicon content in SiNPs was measured by an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

The SiNPs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. First, 0.4 g of sodium citrate was dissolved in 8 mL of ultrapure water with continuous stirring under argon protection for 10 min. Then, 2 mL of N-APTES was added, and stirring was continued for 15 min. Next, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 3 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the product was transferred to a dialysis bag (1 kDa, molecular weight cut-off) for 24 h and then filtered with a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filter to obtain the SiNPs solution (the silicon content measured by ICP-OES was 134.8 μg/mL), which was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for later use. The expression of the content of SiNPs in this paper refers to the content of the silicon element. In addition, a portion of the purified SiNPs solution was freeze-dried, then ground and pulverized into fine powder for partial characterization.
In the control experiment, the situation of using only N-APTES or sodium citrate as the precursor to synthesize materials was examined. The remaining steps were the same as above.

Ratiometric fluorescence detection: First, 100 μL of SiNPs solution (33.7 μg/mL), 100 μL of HCl solution (25 mM), 100 μL of OPD solution (6.4 mM) and different volumes of NO2 − standard solution (1.6 mM) were added to the centrifuge tube in turn. The solution was diluted to 0.5 mL with ultrapure water and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Then 100 μL of NaOH solution (25 mM) and 1 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (0.04 M, pH = 10) were added. The solution was transferred to the quartz cuvette for fluorescence spectrum measurement. The excitation wavelength was set to 410 nm.

Smartphone imaging detection: First, 100 μL of SiNPs solution (4.2 μg/mL), HCl solution (25 mM), and OPD solution (6.4 mM) and different volumes of NO2 − standard solution (1.6 mM) were added sequentially. Then the solution was diluted to 0.5 mL with ultrapure water and incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Then 1.1 mL of H2O was added and the solution was placed in a dark box. The light emitted by the solution after illuminating by the Light Emitting Diode (LED) (10 W, peak wavelength of 402 nm, full width at half maxima (FWHM) of about 12 nm) was captured by the smartphone camera after passing through the filter. The filter only allows light in the 460–1100 nm range to pass through and can effectively block the light emitted from the LED lamp. The captured images were analyzed by the smartphone App (Color Identifier) to get the RGB values, and then the (R + G)/B values were calculated. All images were taken by the OPPO Reno 5 smartphone.

The anti-interference ability and selectivity of the sensing system were evaluated by adding potential interferents at five times the NO2 − concentration in the presence and absence of NO2 − under the above operating conditions. All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NO2 − in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the feasibility of the established detection method for the detection of real samples (tap water, lake water, pickles, sausages), spiked recovery experiments were conducted. The tap water was obtained from our laboratory, and the lake water was obtained from Xinkai Lake of Nankai University. Firstly, the water samples were centrifuged (12000 rpm, 15 min), and the supernatant obtained was then filtered through a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filtration membrane. Pickles and sausages were purchased from a local supermarket (Tianjin, China) and pre-treated according to the National Food Safety Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 5009.33—2016) with slight modifications. First, 5.00 g of sample was weighed to prepare a homogenized sample and placed into a 250 mL conical flask. Then, 12.5 mL of saturated sodium tetraborate solution (50 g/L) was added, followed by 150 mL of water at about 70 °C. After mixing, the sample was heated in a boiling water bath for 15 min, then cooled to room temperature. Another 5 mL of potassium ferrocyanide solution (106 g/L) was added and shaken well, followed by 5 mL of zinc acetate solution (220 g/L). The above solutions were transferred to a 250 mL volumetric flask, fixed and shaken well, and the supernatant was filtered through filter paper after standing for 30 min. The first 30 mL of the filtrate was discarded. The remaining filtrate was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.
After determining the original NO2 − concentration in the real sample, different concentrations of exogenous NO2 − were added and determined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of synthesis conditions of the SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the optimal fluorescence performance, the effects of sodium citrate dosage, reaction temperature, and reaction time on the fluorescence of the SiNPs were investigated with a fixed N-APTES volume of 2 mL. As shown in Fig. S1A, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of the SiNPs gradually increased with the increase of sodium citrate dosage from 0.1 g to 0.4 g and then weakened with a further increase in dosage. The variation of FL intensity with reaction temperature is shown in Fig. S1B, where the FL intensity of the SiNPs was gradually enhanced as the temperature increased from 170 °C to 200 °C, reaching the maximum value at 200 °C. The effect of reaction time is shown in Fig. S1C, with the highest FL intensity observed at a reaction time of 3 h. In summary, 0.4 g, 200 °C, and 3 h were selected as the dosage of sodium citrate, reaction temperature, and time.

In control experiments, sodium citrate, N-APTES, and a mixture of sodium citrate and N-APTES were reacted under the same experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. S2, a clear fluorescence emission peak was observed only when N-APTES and sodium citrate were involved in the reaction together. This suggests that N-APTES as a silicon source and sodium citrate as a reducing agent are necessary to synthesize strongly fluorescent SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The SiNPs were prepared as shown in Scheme 1A. The SiNPs were synthesized in one step by a simple hydrothermal method using N-APTES as the silicon source and sodium citrate as the reducing agent. The simplicity of this synthesis method and the low cost of the required raw materials provide favorable conditions for the large-scale production of SiNPs.

Then the synthesized SiNPs were systematically characterized. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) images showed that the SiNPs were well-dispersed and uniformly spherical ( Fig. 1 A and S3A), indicating successful synthesis with consistent morphology. Two hundred randomly selected particles from the TEM images were counted to obtain a histogram of the size distribution, which indicated that the average particle size of the SiNPs was 2.30 nm. In addition, no clear lattice fringes were observed in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. S3B), and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern exhibited a broad peak centered at 2θ = 20° (Fig. 1B), suggesting that the SiNPs were predominantly in the amorphous phase. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) showed that the SiNPs consisted of C, O, N, and Si ( Fig. 2A), which confirms that the SiNPs were successfully synthesized with the intended elemental composition, providing a basic confirmation of the presence of key components. The functional groups on the surface of the SiNPs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 2B). The broad peak at 3270 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H/O–H [45]. The peaks at 2927 cm−1 and 2873 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibration of C–H [46]. The absorption peak at 1552 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of N–H [47]. The two peaks at 1373 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C−N and Si−O−Si [48,49], while the peak at 908 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of Si–O in the Si–O–H group [50]. The peak at 746 cm−1 indicated the existence of Si–C [49]. These functional groups indicate the successful surface functionalization of the SiNPs.

In addition, the molecular structure and elemental composition of the SiNPs were further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 2 C). The spectrum demonstrated five major peaks at Si 2 p (101.9 eV), Si 2 s (153.0 eV), C 1 s (285.0 eV), N 1 s (400.1 eV), and O 1 s (531.3 eV), confirming that the SiNPs were mainly composed of C, N, O, and Si, which was consistent with the EDS results. The high resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3C) indicated the presence of C–Si (284.0 eV), C–C/CC (284.7 eV), C–N/C–O (285.9 eV), and CO (287.7 eV) [51,52]. The two fitted peaks at 399.0 and 401.0 eV in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3D) were attributed to N–C and N–H [53]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3E) showed three fitted peaks at 530.7, 531.9, and 533.4 eV, corresponding to Si–O, CO, and C–O, respectively [54,55]. The XPS spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. 2D) exhibited three fitted peaks, which were ascribed to Si–C (101.6 eV), Si–N (102.0 eV), and Si–O (102.7 eV) [56], confirming the surface chemistry and the successful integration of these bonds into the SiNPs. The analytical results of XPS were in agreement with those of FT-IR, and these results indicated that the surface of the SiNPs was enriched with hydrophilic groups such as –NH2. Additionally, when the SiNPs were introduced into ethyl acetate-water and water-trichloromethane systems, as shown in Fig. S4, they gradually migrated and dispersed into the aqueous phase, exhibiting blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation, further confirming the excellent water solubility of the SiNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of the SiNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the SiNPs were then investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 3A, the absorption peaks at 240 and 360 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the SiNPs were attributed to π–π * transition and n–π * transition, respectively [57]. The inset shows that the SiNPs dispersed in ultrapure water appear pale yellow in daylight and fluoresce blue under 365 nm UV light. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of the SiNPs are 366 nm and 448 nm. In addition, the emission spectra of the SiNPs exhibit excitation-independence (Fig. 3B), a property related to the homogeneous size and surface state of the SiNPs [58]. At the optimal excitation wavelength, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the SiNPs was measured to be as high as 70 % (Fig. S5), which is much higher than most of the previously reported SiNPs (Table S1).

In addition, the effects of NaCl concentration, temperature, storage time, light, and solution pH on the fluorescence stability of the SiNPs were studied. Whether increasing the NaCl concentration to 1000 mM (Fig. S6A) or changing the temperature (Fig. S6B), the FL intensity of the SiNPs remained almost unchanged, indicating that the SiNPs have excellent salt tolerance and temperature stability. With the increase of storage time, the FL intensity of the SiNPs remained stable (Fig. S6C). In addition, the FL intensity of the SiNPs remained relatively stable after 60 min of continuous irradiation with 410 nm light, showing good photobleaching resistance (Fig. S6D). Finally, the effect of solution pH on the fluorescence of the SiNPs was investigated (Fig. S6E and F). The results showed a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity and a change in peak position in the pH 2–7 range. When the pH reached 8, the SiNPs showed stable fluorescence behavior. This pH-dependent fluorescence characteristic can be attributed to the change of the SiNPs surface charge caused by protonation-deprotonation [59]. In summary, the SiNPs exhibit excellent optical properties, ensuring their reliability in applications.

Under acidic conditions (pH = 2.5), the addition of NO2 − can lead to the fluorescence quenching of the SiNPs ( Fig. 4A), while OPD can react with NO2 − by diazotization to generate diazonium salt. Then, the diazonium salt continued to react with the unreacted amino group of OPD to form yellow benzotriazole, which emitted yellow fluorescence (Scheme S1) [60–62]. However, weak fluorescence of the benzotriazole was observed under acidic conditions, whereas the fluorescence was significantly enhanced under alkaline conditions (pH = 10.0) (Fig. 4A), which was attributed to the deprotonation process [63]. At the same time, adjusting the solution to alkaline only changed the fluorescence peak position of the SiNPs but did not affect the quenching trend of the SiNPs by NO2 − (Fig. 4A). To improve the detection sensitivity, the SiNPs/OPD system was allowed to respond to NO2 − under acidic conditions first, and then the solution was adjusted to alkaline. As shown in Fig. 4B, with the addition of NO2 −, the blue fluorescence of the SiNPs was quenched, while the yellow fluorescence of the benzotriazole was enhanced, allowing for the establishment of a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for accurate detection of NO2 −. Meanwhile, the CIE (CommissionInternationale d′Eclairage, 1931) coordinates in the absence and presence of NO2 − were (0.1672, 0.2684) and (0.3286, 0.3952), respectively (Fig. S7), showing the potential of establishing a smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform, which can be used for visual monitoring and on-site detection of NO2 −.

It was found that there was a good linear relationship between color information and NO2 − concentration when the smartphone imaging detection was performed under both conditions, keeping acidic (pH = 2.5) (Fig. S8A) and tuning to alkaline (pH = 10.0) (Fig. S8B). And both had similar detection limits, indicating similar sensitivity. At the same time, there was a clear color change in both conditions, both of which can be used for visual detection. Therefore, in order to simplify the steps and reduce the complexity of solution configuration and addition, we chose to maintain the acidic conditions for smartphone imaging detection of NO2 − to avoid some of the operational complexities that existed in some of the previous on-site detection methods. The smartphone was used to identify the RGB values of the solution fluorescence when different concentrations of NO2 − were added, and a linear relationship between (R + G)/B and NO2 − concentration was established to achieve on-site detection. In addition, with the addition of NO2 −, the solution showed a color change from colorless to yellow visible to the naked eye under daylight, which can be used for naked eye qualitative analysis (Fig. S9). In summary, the system integrates naked eye qualitative analysis, smartphone visual detection, and precise ratiometric fluorescence detection, providing various feasible solutions for detecting NO2 −.

The detection conditions for ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone imaging were optimized to achieve the best results. To ensure that NO2 − was fully reacted, excessive OPD was introduced, and the concentration of OPD was fixed at 0.4 mM in both detection methods.

Firstly, the ratiometric fluorescence detection was performed, where Ib represents the FL intensity of the SiNPs and Iy represents the FL intensity of benzotriazole. (Ib/Iy) and (Ib/Iy)0 denote the ratios of FL intensities of the two emission peaks in the presence and absence of NO2 −, respectively. The effects of HCl concentration, final pH of the solution, reaction time, excitation wavelength, and the SiNPs concentration on the detection performance were investigated. The results showed that 25 mM was the optimal HCl concentration (Fig. S10A), adjusting the final pH of the solution to 10 had the best effect (Fig. S10B), the reaction reached equilibrium in 10 min (Fig. S10C), and the optimal excitation wavelength was 410 nm (Fig. S10D). In addition, as the SiNPs concentration increased, the linear range of the assay became wider, but the limit of detection (LOD) also increased (Fig. S11). For example, 8.4 μg/mL of the SiNPs (Fig. S11A) resulted in the lowest LOD and narrowest linear range. In contrast, 134.8 μg/mL of the SiNPs (Fig. S11E) provided the widest linear range but also exhibited the highest LOD, which could not satisfy the need for sensitive detection of trace NO2 − content (≤10 μM) in food [64]. Therefore, considering the LOD, linear range, and practical application scenarios, 33.7 μg/mL of the SiNPs with a lower LOD (3σ/0.83 = 0.016 μM) and a wider linear range (0.05–60 μM) was chosen as the optimum SiNPs concentration to balance the detection sensitivity and linear range.

Secondly, the smartphone sensing platform was investigated to study the effects of HCl concentration, SiNPs concentration, and reaction time on detection, and plots of (R + G)/B versus NO2 − concentration were obtained under different conditions. As shown in Fig. S12, the slope (k) was maximum when the HCl concentration was 25 mM, indicating the minimum LOD (3σ/k), so 25 mM was chosen as the optimal HCl concentration. In addition, as shown in Fig. S13, k gradually decreased with the increase of the SiNPs concentration, which implied that the LOD gradually increased. Meanwhile, the inset showed the luminescence change of the solution after adding different concentrations of NO2 − under the illumination of the LED lamp. It can be seen that when the SiNPs concentration is 4.2 μg/mL, the brightness is appropriate and the color change is obvious. Considering the LOD, brightness, and the noticeable degree of color change, 4.2 μg/mL of the SiNPs was selected as the optimal SiNPs concentration. As shown in Fig. S14, the value of (R + G)/B reached equilibrium after 10 min of reaction, so 10 min was selected as the reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone imaging for NO2 − detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were collected at different NO2 − concentrations under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the increase of NO2 − concentration, the FL intensity of the SiNPs (Ib) gradually weakened, while the FL intensity of the benzotriazole (Iy) gradually enhanced, resulting in a gradual decrease of the Ib/Iy ratio. (Ib/Iy)0/(Ib/Iy) and NO2 − concentration showed a good linear relationship, following the linear regression equation (Ib/Iy)0/(Ib/Iy) = 0.8304c + 1.0219 (R2 = 0.998) in the range of 0.05–60.0 μM, where c denotes the NO2 − concentration (Fig. 5B). Based on the 3σ/k principle (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples and k is the slope of the calibration plot), the LOD of the method for NO2 − was calculated to be 0.016 μM. Therefore, this ratiometric fluorescence quantification method for NO2 − is promising to be applied to the determination of NO2 − in various types of food and environmental water samples.

Interestingly, with the increase of NO2 − concentration, the fluorescence of the solution showed a clear color change from blue to yellow ( Fig. 6A). Therefore, we established a smartphone sensing platform for visual quantitative detection of NO2 −. Firstly, a series of fluorescence images were taken by the smartphone (Fig. 6B), followed by identifying and extracting the RGB values of the image colors through the Color Identifier App (Fig. 6C). Then the corresponding (R + G)/B values were calculated. Finally, the linear relationship between (R + G)/B value and NO2 − concentration was established (Fig. 6D). As shown in Fig. 6D, (R + G)/B and NO2 − concentration showed a good linear relationship, following the linear regression equation (R + G)/B = 0.0291c + 0.7330 (R2 = 0.998) in the range of 5–100 μM, where c denotes the NO2 − concentration, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.64 μM. Therefore, this portable smartphone-assisted visual detection platform has broad application prospects in the field detection of NO2 −.

Compared to other recently reported NO2 − sensors (Table S2), the SiNPs/OPD system offers several advantages, including dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence response, high fluorescence quantum yield, short response time, low detection limit, and the convenience of on-site detection via smartphone imaging. These features render the SiNPs/OPD system an attractive option for detecting NO2 − in real water and food samples.

In order to verify the advantage of the SiNPs/OPD system over the SiNPs single-signal fluorescence detection of NO2 −, the SiNPs single-signal sensing was carried out as a control study. As shown in Fig. S15A, with the addition of NO2 −, the FL intensity of the SiNPs gradually decreased. (F0 −F)/F0 showed a good linear relationship with NO2 − concentration in the range of 0.4–10 μM (R2 = 0.998), where F and F0 represent the FL intensity of the SiNPs at 480 nm with or without NO2 −, respectively (Fig. S15B). The calculated LOD was 0.13 μM, which is much higher than that of the SiNPs/OPD system. Meanwhile, as shown in the inset in Fig. S15A, with the addition of NO2 −, only the intensity of the luminescence of the solution can be seen, and no obvious color change can be observed. Therefore, the design of ratiometric fluorescence significantly improves the detection sensitivity, extends the linear range, and also creates conditions for visual detection.

Common cations (Na+, K+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Al3+), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, CH3COO−, NO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, HSO3 −, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−) and small biomolecules (Glu, AA, urea, glucose) were tested. As shown in Fig. 7, only NO2 − (40 μM) could elicit a significant response, and all other interfering substances (200 μM) had negligible effects on both ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone imaging detection, demonstrating the excellent selectivity of the sensing system. The anti-interference ability of the sensing system was evaluated by detecting NO2 − (40 μM) in the presence or absence of other interfering substances (200 μM). The results showed that relative intensities ((Ib/Iy)0/(Ib/Iy)) and relative values ((R + G)/B) were almost the same with and without interfering substances. The effects of the interfering substances could be ignored, confirming that the sensing system has excellent anti-interference ability. These excellent performances are attributed to the fact that the method is based on the specific diazotization reaction of the sensing system with NO2 −.

In addition, to study the repeatability of the ratiometric fluorescence detection method, we used different batches of synthesized SiNPs and the same batch of SiNPs at different times (including intra-day and inter-day) to determine NO2 −. As shown in Fig. S16A, NO2 − was determined using different batches of SiNPs, and the results were almost consistent with a relative standard deviation of 1.18 %. Within a single day and under the same experimental conditions, five repeated measurements of NO2 − at concentrations of 10, 20, and 40 μM were performed (Fig. S16B), and measurements were also performed over five consecutive days (Fig. S16C). The relative standard deviations were calculated to evaluate the intra-day and inter-day precision. The results, as shown in Table S3, confirmed the high precision of the sensing system. All results show that the constructed sensing system has great potential in practical applications.

The SiNPs/OPD sensing system involves changes in two fluorescence signals. Given that there is no interaction between the SiNPs and OPD in the system (Fig. S17A) and that they do not affect each other’s fluorescence (Fig. S17B), the changes in these two fluorescence signals are driven by different mechanisms. Therefore, the mechanism can be discussed in two parts.

The first is the enhancement of the yellow fluorescence. OPD itself is not fluorescent and only reacts with NO2 − under acidic conditions to form a fluorescent product, the fluorescence of which is greatly enhanced under alkaline conditions ( Fig. 8A). A theoretical spectrum was generated by physically superposing the UV–vis absorption spectra of OPD and NO2 − in HCl. However, the experimental spectrum obtained by the actual reaction of OPD and NO2 − in HCl was different from the theoretical spectrum (Fig. 8B), which verified that OPD and NO2 − did react to produce a new substance. A new absorption peak appeared at 450 nm, which was also the reason for the yellowing of the solution. As described in Section 3.4, OPD reacted with NO2 − under acidic conditions to form the yellow fluorescent benzotriazole. Thus, the yellow fluorescence gradually increased with the addition of NO2 −.

The second is the quenching of the blue fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 9A, NO2 − can quench the fluorescence of the SiNPs only under acidic conditions. Moreover, with the increase of NO2 − concentration, the emission peak blue shifted from 480 nm to 475 nm, indicating that the surface structure or conjugation degree of the SiNPs has changed [64]. In addition, the theoretical spectrum obtained by physically superimposing the respective UV–vis absorption spectra of the SiNPs and NO2 − in HCl did not agree with the experimental spectrum (Fig. 9B), indicating that the SiNPs interacted with NO2 − to form a new substance or structure [65], and it was speculated that the diazotization of –NH2 on the surface of the SiNPs with NO2 − occurred [45]. For further verification, the change of zeta potential was also investigated, and the zeta potentials of the SiNPs in HCl in the absence and presence of NO2 − were 28.2 mV and 35.8 mV, respectively (Fig. 9C). The potential of the SiNPs in the presence of NO2 − was higher, possibly due to the diazotization reaction of the SiNPs with NO2 − to generate positively charged diazonium salts [45]. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 9D, the addition of NO2 − did not cause the aggregation of the SiNPs, thus excluding the possibility of aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching. At the same time, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NO2 − did not overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of the SiNPs (Fig. 9E), and the fluorescence lifetimes of the SiNPs did not change with the addition of NO2 − (Fig. 9F), which ruled out the possibility of inner filter effect (IFE) and FRET. In general, the absorption spectrum of the SiNPs changed while the fluorescence lifetime remained almost unchanged, so the fluorescence quenching of the SiNPs triggered by NO2 − can be attributed to the static quenching caused by the formation of a stable non-fluorescent complex between NO2 − and the surface functional groups of the SiNPs [23]. Meanwhile, we observed that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of benzotriazole overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the SiNPs ( Fig. 10A). The fluorescence lifetime of the SiNPs decreased with the generation of benzotriazole (Fig. 10B). Therefore, it was speculated that there was a FRET between benzotriazole and the SiNPs, leading to further fluorescence quenching of the SiNPs. In summary, the quenching of the SiNPs was attributed to static quenching and FRET.

In the ratiometric fluorescence detection, the solution was adjusted to alkaline when the reaction was completed, which only affected the position of the emission peaks of the SiNPs (Fig. S6F) and the FL intensity of the benzotriazole. The fluorescence change trend was determined by the reaction occurring under acidic conditions, so the mechanism part was discussed under acidic conditions.

In order to verify the practicability of the proposed method, the NO2 − contents in tap water, Xinkai Lake water, pickles, and sausages were measured. After measuring the NO2 − originally present in the real samples, spike recovery experiments were performed. The results, as shown in Table S4, showed that the spiked recoveries ranged from 98.4 % to 112.1 % for the ratiometric fluorescence detection method and 93.0 % to 104.3 % for the smartphone sensing platform, which indicates the potential application of the method for NO2 − detection in the environment and food.

However, there is still room for improvement in this study. Real water and food samples may require filtration, dilution, or other preprocessing steps, which could alter the concentration of NO2 − and lead to deviations in the final results. To solve this problem, automated sample preparation equipment could be introduced in the future, as it would reduce operating errors and ensure sample consistency. Furthermore, although smartphone imaging technology is portable and cost-effective, the accuracy of imaging and analysis software is limited. If higher-resolution cameras or imaging algorithms are introduced in the future, the detection limit could be further reduced. In our follow-up research, we will actively explore these directions to achieve further improvements.

In summary, the SiNPs with high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent performance were synthesized in one step by a simple and easy hydrothermal method. Based on the changes in fluorescence signal and fluorescence color caused by the diazo coupling reaction of the SiNPs/OPD system with NO2 −, we innovatively proposed a ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone imaging strategy for detecting NO2 −. Notably, the dual-reverse-signal ratiometric fluorescence detection strategy for NO2 − showed many advantages, amplifying the signal while eliminating the background interference, thus significantly improving the sensitivity and reliability of the detection. More importantly, both detection methods have shown excellent feasibility and practicality in detecting NO2 − in real samples. This work not only provides a method for the preparation of excellent SiNPs but also offers a new way for the sensitive detection of NO2 −, which provides ideas for the detection of other pollutants in the environment and food.

Nitrite (NO2 −) is commonly found in the environment and food, but its excessive accumulation poses a serious threat to human health and the ecological environment. In order to detect NO2 − in complex matrices, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor with dual reverse signals was constructed by combining ultrabright silicon nanoparticles with o-phenylenediamine using a post-mixing strategy. Meanwhile, a smartphone-assisted visualization platform was developed for sensitive detection of NO2 −. Overall, this method shows broad application potential in monitoring NO2 − in the environment and food.
